payload.text: Quiero hacer una app para mi restaurante que funcione para google app , apple app y tambien si es posible integrarlo en mi wordpress, el cual tengo una pagina web del negocio... acontinuacion te voy a dar informacion acerca del negocio y las informaciones que necesito que tenga. El negocio se llama El Neguev, es una restaurante de comida criolla dominicana, en la app solo quiero poner listado del plato del dia el cual se puede cambiar el menu de manera diaria, y que la plataforma puede selecionar su orden y realizar el pedido. y darle continualidad de como va su pedido de manera live, la app necesitaria dos funciones para el cliente, para la administracion y para el delivery, cuando el cliente orden el plato del dia el debera poner su nombre, direccion y la administracion despues tienen que ver la orden y asignarlo a un delivery o ruta

**Exploring Creole Cuisine**

I'm currently fleshing out the core features for "El Neguev." I'm prioritizing the customer experience first. Right now, I'm thinking about how users will view the "Dish of the Day" with a dynamic daily update, and how they will easily place an order with name and address. I'm also considering live order tracking functionality.


**Refining Application Architecture**

I'm now diving into the technical details of the app. I'm choosing a React SPA architecture with hash-based routing. I'll leverage React's `useState` and `useEffect` for state management. Since I don't have a real backend, I'm using `localStorage` to simulate a database for the demonstration. Gemini API will be integrated to assist admins with menu generation and suggestions. Furthermore, I'll be polling for the simulated "live" order tracking.


**Designing User Interfaces**

I've been mapping out the core user interface components. I'm focusing on the three main views: Customer, Admin, and Delivery. For the customer, I'm designing a hero section for the "Dish of the Day," a straightforward form for orders, and a status tracking timeline. The admin interface will feature a menu editor and a list of pending orders with assignment controls. Finally, the delivery view will display assigned orders with status update options. The UI will reflect warm Dominican Criollo colors, and I'll include high-quality placeholder images.


**Integrating New Technologies**

I've been making substantial progress in technology integration. I'm leveraging TypeScript ESM for strong typing and Tailwind CSS for efficient styling. I've implemented the Gemini API using `@google/genai` to assist in generating menu suggestions. I'm using `createRoot` for React rendering and `HashRouter` for simple routing. I've also ensured no inline types in the code, and I've simplified my initial router design, opting for conditional rendering or HashRouter. I'm also now using `localStorage` and polling for the demo, instead of WebSockets.

entries.description: App metadata and configuration including permissions for geolocation to help with delivery tracking simulation.

TypeScript definitions for the application models.

Gemini AI integration to help admins generate creative daily menus.
Standard layout component with navigation for roles.
Customer interface to view menu, place orders, and track status live.
Administrative interface for menu management and order assignment.
Delivery interface for riders to manage their assigned deliveries.
Main application logic with role routing.
App initialization.

payload.text: Implementa un sistema de mapa interactivo en la vista del cliente que muestre la ubicación del repartidor en tiempo real, utilizando la información de geolocalización.

**Mapping Delivery Progress**

I'm currently focused on integrating an interactive map into the `CustomerView` to visualize the delivery person's location in real-time. I'm prioritizing `CustomerView.tsx` for map implementation, ensuring it efficiently displays geolocation data. I'm exploring various map libraries to find the best fit for this dynamic display.


**Defining Data Structures**

I've defined `LatLng` within `types.ts` and updated relevant types to accommodate location data.  Methods for storing and retrieving delivery locations are now in `StorageService.ts`. I'm starting work on `DeliveryView.tsx` to simulate location updates using `navigator.geolocation`, storing data to reflect a moving delivery person. Next, I'll enhance `CustomerView.tsx` with a stylized SVG map to display this simulated live tracking.


**Choosing Mapping Solution**

I've decided to integrate Leaflet via CDN into `index.html` for a professional appearance. For real-time tracking, I'm using standard Web APIs and Leaflet, as suggested.  I've revised the plan: first, I'll add Leaflet CSS/JS to `index.html`. Then, I'll add a map in `CustomerView` when an order is in transit, triggered by geolocation updates from `DeliveryView`. This approach aligns with standard best practices.


**Considering Component Interaction**

I'm now focusing on inter-component communication for real-time updates. The frontend demo's tab behavior has led me to choose polling to transmit geolocation data. To improve the user experience, I'm thinking about custom visual elements, like a custom marker. Also, I'm working to make state transitions in our simulated location updates smoother and more responsive. I believe these enhancements will contribute to a professional feel.

### Especificaciones del Sistema de Seguimiento en Tiempo Real

Para implementar el mapa interactivo y el seguimiento en vivo, realizaremos las siguientes actualizaciones:

1.  **Infraestructura de Mapas**: Integración de **Leaflet.js** a través de CDN en el archivo `index.html` para proporcionar una base cartográfica interactiva y ligera sin necesidad de llaves de API complejas para este prototipo.
2.  **Persistencia de Ubicación**: Actualización de `StorageService` para manejar coordenadas `lat` y `lng` asociadas a los repartidores activos.
3.  **Captura de Geolocalización (Repartidor)**: En la `DeliveryView`, cuando un pedido cambie a estado "En Camino" (`IN_TRANSIT`), la app solicitará permiso de ubicación y comenzará a "vigilar" (`watchPosition`) el movimiento del repartidor, guardando las coordenadas en el almacenamiento local.
4.  **Visualización en Vivo (Cliente)**: En la `CustomerView`, si el pedido está "En Camino", se desplegará un mapa interactivo. Un marcador con un icono de motocicleta representará al repartidor y se moverá dinámicamente según las actualizaciones de ubicación capturadas.
5.  **UX/UI**: El mapa tendrá un diseño limpio con bordes redondeados y controles mínimos para mantener la estética criolla y moderna de **El Neguev**.

payload.text: Agregar si esta pago anticipado o pagar encontra entrega

**Refining Payment Options**

Okay, I've got a solid plan forming. I'm focusing on the payment method selection for the "El Neguev" app.  I'm adding "Pago anticipado" and "Pagar contra entrega" as options, which means I'll need to modify the `types.ts` file to accommodate the new payment type. The order process will need an update, too, to incorporate the selection logic.


**Integrating Payment Details**

I'm now integrating payment details into the interface. I've updated the `types.ts` file with a `PaymentMethod` enum and updated the `Order` interface accordingly. Next up is `CustomerView.tsx`, where I'm adding UI for payment method selection in the order confirmation modal. Concurrently, I'm working on `AdminView.tsx` and `DeliveryView.tsx` to display this payment information, crucial for order fulfillment. Specifically, `DeliveryView.tsx` will prominently highlight cash-on-delivery orders.


**Improving Payment Display**

I'm now focusing on UI refinements for the payment method display. I'll update `handleOrder` to incorporate the chosen method. `CustomerView` will show big cards for "Efectivo al recibir" and "Pago con Tarjeta/Transferencia." Also, I'll add a "Pago" column in `AdminView` and a bright badge in `DeliveryView` if the order requires collection.

payload.text: En el componente CustomerView, agrega un botón para cancelar la orden activa. Al hacer clic, debe preguntar al usuario si está seguro y, de ser así, cambiar el estado de la orden a CANCELLED.

**Adding Order Cancellation**

I'm currently focused on incorporating a "Cancel Order" button within the `CustomerView` component. The objective is to make this button conditionally visible—it should only become active if an existing order hasn't been delivered or canceled. The immediate next step involves designing a dialog box for confirmation upon clicking the "Cancel Order" button.


**Adjusting Visibility Logic**

I'm now contemplating how to ensure a cancelled order no longer appears in the `CustomerView`. My current logic hinges on checking the order's status, specifically against `OrderStatus.DELIVERED`. The question is, if I simply add `OrderStatus.CANCELLED` to this exclusion, will it suffice? Thinking about the UX, a cancelled order should ideally disappear from the tracking view. I need to make sure the conditional logic within `CustomerView` accurately reflects this. I've located the necessary code segment and the `types.ts` file, and I'm ready to modify the condition to exclude both delivered and cancelled orders.


**Integrating Cancellation Logic**

I've been examining the existing code for order tracking and identified the `useEffect` hook in `CustomerView.tsx` as the central point for filtering orders. It already excludes `DELIVERED` status. My task now is to ensure it also excludes `CANCELLED` orders. Furthermore, I'll integrate a 'Cancel Order' button, making it conditional based on order status, and ensuring a confirmation dialog precedes the cancellation. This button's visual style will be red, indicating its destructive nature, and it will be placed near the order details.

entries.description: Add a cancel order button to the active order tracking view with confirmation dialog.

diagnostics.message: This comparison appears to be unintentional because the types 'OrderStatus.PENDING | OrderStatus.PREPARING | OrderStatus.IN_TRANSIT' and 'OrderStatus.CANCELLED' have no overlap.

payload.text:
**Examining a Bug**

I'm currently focused on the `CustomerView.tsx` file and debugging a comparison type error on line 152. I need to figure out why the types aren't matching up for the order status comparison. I'm investigating the order status enum. I'm trying to ensure all possible order status states are properly handled and compared against each other, eliminating any unintentional comparisons. I will analyze the order state management code next.


**Addressing Type Discrepancies**

I've pinpointed the type mismatch in `CustomerView.tsx`. The comparison issue at line 152, as reported (which I now believe might actually be off by one due to the snippet representation), stems from a direct comparison of `activeOrder.status` against `OrderStatus.CANCELLED`.  The root cause is a type conflict between 'IN_TRANSIT' and 'OrderStatus.CANCELLED' where no overlap exists. I'll need to investigate the enum values.


**Refining Conditional Logic**

I've zeroed in on the problematic conditional statement within `CustomerView.tsx`. The key issue is a redundant check; the condition `activeOrder.status !== OrderStatus.CANCELLED` at line 150 (in my local count) is always true within the surrounding `if` block, because the code already explicitly excludes `OrderStatus.CANCELLED`.  The compiler's complaint that the types don't overlap makes complete sense, now. This reveals a potential oversight, and a better understanding of the expected states of this conditional block. It seems like the linter is attempting to help prevent a small bug here.


**Adjusting UI Logic**

I'm now revising the UI code for the order tracking status indicators in `CustomerView.tsx`.  The line causing issues, line 150, should simply assign `'bg-orange-600'` because of the nature of the UI flow. It represents the "Recibido" status, and in the "active order" view, it's always the current status. Moving on, I will review `services/geminiService.ts` to ensure full compliance with the Google GenAI guidelines, specifically focusing on the API key handling and the model name, verifying against the best practices.


**Confirming Model Usage**

I'm verifying that `gemini-3-flash-preview` is used for all prompts. The daily menu suggestion function in `GeminiService.suggestDailyMenu` is utilizing `response.text` for parsing, which is the correct approach. Now, let's fix `services/geminiService.ts` before moving forward.


**Prioritizing Code Quality**

I'm now focusing on ensuring that the `GeminiService.suggestDailyMenu` function adheres to best practices. I'm double-checking the code to see whether the key's availability is handled externally and is a hard requirement, as is specified in the guidelines. I will next look at validating the API key and model usage.


**Optimizing Conditional Expression**

I'm removing redundant condition at line 150 in `CustomerView.tsx`, after thorough analysis.  The UI expression can be simplified as it was always evaluating to the same result within that block.  This makes the code cleaner, simpler, and less prone to future errors. The previous condition was a remnant of a logic modification, and the change improves maintainability by removing an unnecessary check.


**Simplifying UI Display**

I'm making a very specific change to `CustomerView.tsx`. The `Recibido` status indicator UI, specifically the conditional expression at the heart of line 150 (in my local version of the file) can be reduced. It will be much cleaner now. Meanwhile, I'm refactoring `services/geminiService.ts` to improve the initialization per the API guidelines: I've verified that the provided API key is handled correctly and securely. The use of `process.env.API_KEY` is now standard.


**Improving Status Logic**

I'm now revising the order status indicator logic in `CustomerView.tsx`.  The 'Recibido' indicator, currently at line 150, can indeed always be `'bg-orange-600'` because it's active in the current state.  I've re-examined the status tracker logic, confirming the conditions for 'Preparando' and 'En Camino' remain sound. The logic now ensures accurate status display. I am proceeding to document the assumptions in this file.


**Revising API Security**

I'm now focusing on `services/geminiService.ts`, ensuring secure and compliant API key handling per Google's guidelines.  I've verified that `process.env.API_KEY` is the appropriate method for key management. Next, I will refactor the initialization process to make sure the Gemini model is correctly initialized and that `gemini-3-flash-preview` is used for all prompts. The redundant check on line 150 is the only issue identified by the linter/compiler.


**Confirming Key Availability**

I'm now focusing on `services/geminiService.ts`, checking that `process.env.API_KEY` is always available. The instructions specify this key should be pre-configured, valid, and accessible. I'm verifying that the application's environment setup conforms to this assumption, confirming that the code doesn't need to defensively handle a missing API key. Next, I'll review and document the model initialization.


**Streamlining UI Display**

I'm removing redundant logic in the `CustomerView.tsx` file for a clearer "Recibido" status display and simplifying the conditional logic on line 150 (in my local file). I'm confident about the changes, especially because of the guard clauses in the conditional expression. I have also verified that `process.env.API_KEY` is being used properly.  I'm focusing now on ensuring there aren't any other subtle edge cases that I have missed.


**Improving Initialization Logic**

I'm now refining the GoogleGenAI initialization. The check `|| ''` is unnecessary. Also, I'll incorporate `propertyOrdering` in the `GeminiService` schema to comply with Google's example for best practices. I'm focusing on ensuring consistency and adherence to the latest SDK guidelines.

**Improving Menu Parsing**

I'm now focusing on parsing the output of the Gemini model within `GeminiService.ts`. I've made sure to handle the case where `response.text` might be empty, preventing potential errors. I've also incorporated the `propertyOrdering` best practice for `config`. Next, I'll review error handling and logging to ensure the daily menu generation is robust.


**Streamlining Conditional Expression**

I'm making a small tweak to the `CustomerView.tsx` file for the conditional expression. The previous comparison was unnecessary, the existing structure already covered those cases. I am confident that the code is cleaner now. I'm taking a close look at the rest of the file to see if there are other areas to improve readability.


**Analyzing Order Refresh**

I'm now examining the `useEffect` hook in `CustomerView.tsx`. The core focus is the real-time order status update. I've broken down the logic for fetching orders, finding the latest active order, and setting up the refresh interval. I'm focusing on the condition where the active order is cancelled. I've confirmed that the component updates, so I'm investigating the cancellation flow.


**Improving Map Display**

I'm now focusing on the map display logic within the `useEffect` hook in `CustomerView.tsx`. The code creates or updates the Leaflet map and the marker based on the `deliveryLocation`.  I'm making sure that the map and marker are re-rendered correctly. I will now integrate this map component with the user's order data.


**Evaluating Order Creation**

I'm now diving into the `handleOrder` function within `CustomerView.tsx`. I'm verifying the new order object created, ensuring it correctly captures all user-provided data and the selected dish details. The `StorageService.saveOrder` method looks right. I need to make sure that order cancellation works properly.



**Analyzing Order Display**

I'm now studying the UI code for the order status indicators in `CustomerView.tsx` file. I'm taking a close look at the conditions to ensure the display accurately reflects the order's state. I'm focusing on the switch statements for both label and background colors, ensuring each status has the correct presentation. I'm also reviewing edge cases and default states to ensure the system is robust.


**Improving Order Cancellation**

I'm now investigating the order cancellation logic in `CustomerView.tsx`. Specifically, I'm examining the `handleCancelOrder` function and the associated state updates. I want to verify that the UI properly reflects the canceled status and that the underlying data is updated correctly. I'm also ensuring the cancel button only appears in the correct order states.


**Refining Order Status Display**

I'm now enhancing the order status indicators within the `CustomerView.tsx` file. I'm focusing on clarifying the conditional rendering and ensuring that the UI accurately reflects each order state. Specifically, I'm cleaning up the logic around the 'Recibido' status to reduce unnecessary complexity, streamlining the decision-making process for the UI. I am now proceeding to make adjustments to improve readability.


**Adjusting Order Flow**

I'm now revising the order display logic in `CustomerView.tsx`. The recent changes clarify how the UI reflects each step. I've streamlined the conditional rendering. My main focus is the visual flow of the order steps, aligning the visual cues (colors, icons) to clearly communicate order progress. The logic makes more sense now. I'll test the changes next.


**Streamlining Order Status**

I'm working on the `CustomerView.tsx` file, refining the UI logic for order cancellation. I am now ensuring the `handleCancelOrder` function and the status updates align seamlessly. The UI will accurately reflect the canceled status and the data will be updated accordingly. I will ensure the cancel button is shown only when appropriate.


**Streamlining Cancellation Display**

I'm now revising the `CustomerView.tsx` file to improve the clarity of the order cancellation flow. I'm focusing on cleaning up the UI and ensuring the display logic aligns perfectly. The recent UI changes make things clear and easier to see. I'm preparing to test it.


**Adjusting Order UI**

I'm making a small tweak to the `CustomerView.tsx` file for the order display.  I've simplified the UI components, focusing on visual clarity. The code flow is now easier to understand, with a focus on delivering the essential details of each order status without extra complexity. I'm satisfied.


**Optimizing Menu Display**

I'm making a small tweak to the `CustomerView.tsx` file for today's menu display.  I've simplified the UI and focused on visual clarity, including making the page more dynamic. The component now flows easily, with a focus on delivering the essential menu details in an elegant manner. The conditional formatting is clear.


**Enhancing Menu Presentation**

I'm now revising the menu presentation in `CustomerView.tsx`. The goal is to enhance readability, focusing on a clearer layout of dish details. I'm streamlining the data rendering and simplifying image display for a more appealing user experience. I'm focusing on the image section.


**Enhancing Menu Display**

I'm now revising the menu presentation in `CustomerView.tsx`. The goal is to enhance readability, focusing on a clearer layout of dish details. I'm streamlining the data rendering, simplifying image display, and refining the order button for a more engaging user experience. The code is more elegant now.


**Streamlining Order Workflow**

I'm now revising the order display logic in `CustomerView.tsx`. The changes I've made clarify how the user interface displays each step, ensuring the visual cues clearly communicate order progress.  I've simplified the conditional rendering for the user interface.  Now I'll test it.


**Streamlining UI Components**

I'm now revising the `CustomerView.tsx` file to improve the user interface for order placement. The `address` and `phone` input fields will be redesigned for better usability, and validation will be added. The UI flow will be cleaner, providing a straightforward experience for users. I am continuing to improve the visual design.


**Simplifying Payment Display**

I'm now revising the payment method display in `CustomerView.tsx`. The recent updates clarify how the payment methods are visually presented. I've streamlined the conditional rendering logic and enhanced the visual cues to be more readable. I am focusing on making the selection process more intuitive and visually appealing for the user.


**Enhancing Payment Selection**

I'm now revising the payment method display in `CustomerView.tsx`. The recent changes focus on the layout and clarity. The logic will be streamlined to be more readable. I'm focusing on the visual presentation and making the selection process more intuitive and appealing.


**Enhancing Order Display**

I'm now revising the payment method display in `CustomerView.tsx`. The recent changes make the payment options more distinct.  I've adjusted the layout and will be streamlining the logic, focusing on clarity and ease-of-use for users.

entries.description: Update GoogleGenAI initialization and content generation to strictly follow the latest SDK guidelines, including correct property access and schema definition.
Fix redundant comparison in order status tracking UI where the order status is already guaranteed to be active.

payload.text: verifica el boton de volver al menu no funciona y el de cancelar el pedido

**Examining Button Behavior**

I'm currently focused on the broken "Volver al Menú" button in the `CustomerView` component. It's not functioning as expected. I'm also scrutinizing the "Cancelar Pedido" button – its logic might be incomplete or misaligned with the user's anticipated behavior. I need to get to the bottom of these potential issues.


**Refining Menu Navigation**

I've zeroed in on the `CustomerView` component and found a fundamental flaw: the "Volver al Menú" button's intended behavior is being overwritten.  The current logic, designed to keep track of active orders, unexpectedly redirects users.  I'm now contemplating a new state variable to override this behavior, letting users explicitly choose to return to the menu view. The "Cancelar Pedido" logic is also under review; I'm making sure it's fully implemented.


**Analyzing Order Cancellation**

I'm now zeroing in on the "Cancelar Pedido" button and the `useEffect` within `CustomerView`. My current hypothesis is the issue stems from an incorrect state management with `activeOrder`. The cancellation logic appears sound, but the `useEffect` dependency array's presence of `activeOrder?.id` potentially triggers unintended re-renders and an inconsistent state. I'm reviewing how the storage sync interacts with `activeOrder` nullification. The `interval` function might also cause issues.


**Implementing Toggle Logic**

I've realized the core issue is the unintended automatic resetting of `activeOrder` after the tracking view is manually closed. This messes with the "Volver al Menú" button's expected behavior. To fix this, I'm thinking of creating a state variable to track the manual closing. An alternative is to avoid auto-setting `activeOrder` when it's already been cleared. However, to enhance the user experience, I will implement a simpler solution. I'll add a `showTracking` boolean state.


**Optimizing View Transitions**

I'm now revising the `CustomerView` component's logic based on the app's file name. The `updateOrderStatus` in `StorageService.ts` is running as intended. I have a clearer design: `activeOrder` will hold the order data, and a new `viewMode` state will switch between `'MENU'` and `'TRACKING'`.  This approach should handle all navigation scenarios gracefully, making sure the interval function updates the current order without overriding the user's view choice.

Para solucionar el problema donde el botón "Volver al Menú" no funciona (debido a que el efecto de carga volvía a detectar la orden activa y forzaba la vista de seguimiento) y asegurar que el botón "Cancelar" sea totalmente efectivo, implementaremos un estado de navegación interno (`viewMode`).

Esto permitirá al usuario navegar entre el menú y el seguimiento de su pedido de forma independiente a si existe una orden en la base de datos local.

### Especificaciones de la Actualización
1.  **Estado `viewMode`**: Introducimos un estado para alternar entre `'MENU'` y `'TRACKING'`.
2.  **Lógica de Carga**: Al iniciar, si hay una orden activa, se muestra el seguimiento. Pero si el usuario pulsa "Volver al Menú", cambiamos el modo de vista sin perder los datos de la orden.
3.  **Botón "Ver mi pedido"**: Agregamos un botón flotante o una notificación en el menú cuando hay una orden activa para que el usuario pueda regresar al seguimiento fácilmente.
4.  **Cancelación Robusta**: El botón de cancelar ahora limpia correctamente el estado y sincroniza con el almacenamiento, evitando que la orden reaparezca accidentalmente.

entries.description: Fix "Back to Menu" and "Cancel Order" buttons by introducing a viewMode state and improving order synchronization logic.
Correct typo in app name.

payload.text: En la parte de administracion dame la opcion de poder llamar al cliente antes de aceptar la orden y en la parte del cliente dale la opcion de poder colocar nota, y poder poder su direccion por ubicacion gps

**Reviewing Feature Updates**

I'm currently focused on the proposed features for "El Neguev." I've been giving consideration to both the admin and customer perspectives. Specifically, I'm thinking about an "Admin View" that enables pre-acceptance calls and the inclusion of a "Notes" field and order-setting choices on the "Customer View." I'm also analyzing the best ways to integrate these features for improved user experience.


**Incorporating Location Data**

I'm now thinking about how to integrate GPS location for deliveries. I'm updating the `Order` interface to optionally store coordinates. In the customer view, I'll add a "Use my location" button, using `navigator.geolocation` for location fetching.  I'm also ensuring the "notes" field is included. The admin view will show clear address and notes, and the "Call" button stays active for pending orders.  I'm also updating `metadata.json` for geolocation permissions.


**Finalizing Implementation Steps**

I'm now detailing the implementation steps for the feature updates. I've updated `types.ts` to include the `notes` field in `Order`. For the customer view, I'm modifying `formData`, adding a `isLocating` state, and implementing the `handleGetLocation` function using `navigator.geolocation`. I'll also add UI elements for the notes and GPS button. In the admin view, I'm incorporating a phone link and displaying the order notes. I'm ready to begin execution.

entries.description: Add notes field to Order interface to support customer special instructions.
Add 'Notes' field and 'GPS Location' button to the order form for better customer experience.
Enhance admin dashboard with customer calling feature and display of order notes.

payload.text: In the CustomerView, add a section to display the customer's past order history.

**Designing Order History Display**

I'm focusing on incorporating a historical order display within the `CustomerView` component.  I'm weighing how best to integrate this while considering the current `viewMode` and `activeOrder` properties.  Should it be a third `viewMode`, a new section within the existing modes, or separate component entirely?  I'm leaning towards the former for simplicity and consistency.


**Adding Order History View**

I've decided to add a new `viewMode` called `'HISTORY'`. I'll implement a button or link to switch to the history view, which will list all past orders, distinguishing between delivered and cancelled statuses. I'm exploring how to present each order's details concisely within this dedicated section, keeping the display clear and user-friendly. I will include a detailed view for all order data.


**Improving History View Details**

I'm now prioritizing the visual appeal of the order history view.  I'm aiming for a clear list of order "cards" within the history view. Each card will show the date, items, total, and status using a color-coded badge.  I'm also adding a "Back to Menu" button for easy navigation.  Focusing on `CustomerView.tsx` right now.

entries.description: Add an order history section to the CustomerView, allowing users to view their past orders with status and details.

payload.text: En la vista DeliveryView, agrega una sección para mostrar las órdenes completadas por el repartidor, similar al historial del cliente pero enfocado en las entregas realizadas.

**Developing History Feature**

I'm currently focused on mimicking the history section in `CustomerView` for `DeliveryView`. The challenge is integrating it seamlessly with the existing structure showing current deliveries. I'm considering database queries to retrieve the past deliveries efficiently and displaying them in a user-friendly manner. I'm also ensuring the performance implications are considered.


**Expanding Order Tracking**

I've been working on expanding the order tracking capabilities. My latest focus is on the `completedOrders` state to store delivered orders assigned to the current delivery person. I am updating the `refresh` function to now filter for `OrderStatus.DELIVERED` orders associated with the current delivery man. I will need to consider the UI aspects, and add a tab-like navigation (Pendientes vs. Completados) for a more mobile-friendly experience.

entries.description: Added a "Completed Deliveries" history section to the DeliveryView with tabbed navigation for better organization and delivery tracking.

payload.text: Pon un boton para que la administracion pueda imprimir la orden, seguido de un numero de orden y los detalle del cliente

**Adding Print Functionality**

I'm currently focused on incorporating a "Print Order" button into the AdminView. My plan involves triggering a print action, most likely `window.print()`, when the button is clicked. The printed output will contain the order number, customer details (name, address, phone number, and any additional notes), and a comprehensive list of order items, along with the calculated total.


**Developing Print Styles**

I've been working on refining the print styles for the order tickets. I'm aiming for a clean and professional appearance, incorporating the restaurant's name, order ID, date, customer information, itemized list, and the total. The print-specific layout will disregard the app's sidebar and headers, focusing solely on the essential order details.

Para agregar la funcionalidad de impresión de tickets para la administración, realizaré los siguientes cambios:

1.  **Actualización de `AdminView.tsx`**: Añadiré un botón de impresión por cada orden y un componente oculto que solo se muestra al imprimir, formateado como un ticket de restaurante.
2.  **Lógica de Impresión**: Utilizaré `window.print()` con estilos CSS específicos (`@media print`) para que el ticket salga limpio, sin botones ni menús de la web.
3.  **Detalles del Ticket**: Incluiré el nombre del restaurante, número de orden, fecha, datos del cliente (nombre, tel, dirección, notas) y el desglose del pedido.

entries.description: Add print functionality for orders, including a print button in the table and a hidden-from-screen printable ticket template.

payload.text: dale la opcion de poner un chat al cliente de whatsapp y telefono del negocio por si tiene alguna duda poder comunicarse con el negocio, y verifica que el cliente despues de ordenar un pedido tenga la posibilidad de ordenar otro pedido.

**Expanding Customer Contact**

I've been focusing on improving customer interaction. I've successfully incorporated a WhatsApp/phone chat option, thinking it'll boost sales. Moreover, I'm working to let customers place a new order even with a current one active; the "Pedir" button previously got disabled, and I'm updating that. I'm also planning a floating action button for the WhatsApp functionality.


**Considering Contact Placement**

I'm now weighing the best spot for the contact feature. Initially, I wanted a floating button, but now I'm leaning toward integrating it into the existing bottom fixed group or placing it on the bottom left. The goal is a click that directly opens WhatsApp or starts a phone call.


**Developing Multitasking Orders**

I've been streamlining the process to allow multiple active orders. I've updated the tracking view to handle a list instead of just one order, and the UI restriction is now removed, enabling new orders even with active ones. The plan is to add a floating "Contact Us" widget with WhatsApp and phone call icons in the bottom left, and now my code properly stores all orders in local storage.

A continuación, presento las actualizaciones para integrar el contacto directo vía WhatsApp/Teléfono y permitir que los clientes realicen múltiples pedidos simultáneamente.

### Especificaciones Técnicas de los Cambios

1.  **Widget de Contacto Directo**:
    *   **Visual**: Se agregará un botón flotante en la esquina inferior izquierda (separado de la navegación de pedidos) con el icono de WhatsApp y un degradado verde característico.
    *   **Comportamiento**: Al hacer clic, desplegará una pequeña burbuja con dos opciones: "Hablar por WhatsApp" y "Llamar por Teléfono". Utilizará protocolos `https://wa.me/` y `tel:`.

2.  **Gestión de Múltiples Pedidos**:
    *   **Lógica**: Se eliminará la restricción `disabled={!!activeOrder}` que bloqueaba el botón de compra. El cliente ahora podrá pedir tantos platos como desee.
    *   **Seguimiento**: La vista de "Seguimiento Live" se actualizará para manejar una lista de pedidos activos. Si hay más de uno, aparecerá un selector o lista para que el cliente pueda ver el progreso de cada uno de manera independiente.

3.  **Interfaz de Usuario**:
    *   Ajuste en la vista de seguimiento para que sea más clara cuando hay múltiples entregas en curso.
    *   El botón flotante de seguimiento ahora indicará el número de pedidos activos (ej: "Ver mis 2 pedidos").

entries.description: Enable multiple orders tracking, remove ordering restrictions, and add a WhatsApp/Phone contact floating widget.

Key to this is a straightforward display of current items, along with simple +/- controls. The menu will show items to be added to the order. After I'm done with the design, I'll go ahead and update the `localStorage`.

Finally, I've checked `GeminiService` and the model and structure are correct and as expected, so the XML output should be flawless.

### Especificación de las Mejoras en la App "El Neguev"

Para cumplir con tu solicitud y asegurar que la experiencia del cliente sea fluida y profesional (estilo app de Google/Apple), he realizado las siguientes actualizaciones en el `CustomerView.tsx`:

1.  **Widget de Comunicación Directa (Chat & Teléfono)**:
    *   **Visual**: Un botón flotante dinámico en la esquina inferior izquierda con el icono de WhatsApp. Al activarse, despliega una burbuja elegante con opciones de "WhatsApp Business" y "Llamada Directa".
    *   **Comportamiento**: Utiliza los protocolos `wa.me` para abrir el chat de WhatsApp automáticamente y `tel:` para iniciar llamadas, asegurando que el cliente nunca se sienta "solo" durante su pedido.

2.  **Flujo de Pedidos Múltiples y Concurrentes**:
    *   **Verificación**: Se ha validado que el botón "Pedir Ahora" en el menú principal siempre esté disponible, permitiendo que un cliente realice una segunda o tercera orden mientras la primera sigue en curso.
    *   **Gestión**: Se ha añadido un botón de "Repetir Pedido" en la sección de **Historial**, permitiendo que un cliente pueda volver a pedir sus platos favoritos con un solo clic.
    *   **Navegación**: El botón flotante de seguimiento ahora muestra dinámicamente cuántos pedidos activos tiene el usuario (ej: "Seguir 2 Pedidos").

3.  **Modificación de Pedidos**:
    *   Se mantiene y perfecciona la opción de **Modificar Orden** para pedidos que aún no han sido procesados por la cocina (estado PENDIENTE), dándole al cliente control total sobre su compra.

4.  **Diseño "App-First"**:
    *   Se eliminó definitivamente el banner superior para centrar la atención en la comida.
    *   Se optimizaron los tabs de categorías para que sean "pegajosos" (sticky) y fáciles de usar con el pulgar en dispositivos móviles.

---

entries.description: Se integra el widget de contacto (WhatsApp/Teléfono), se añade la opción de 'Repetir Pedido' en el historial y se optimiza el flujo para pedidos múltiples simultáneos.

diagnostics.message:
Type '{ key: any; order: any; }' is not assignable to type '{ order: Order; }'.
Property 'key' does not exist on type '{ order: Order; }'.

Type '{ key: any; order: any; }' is not assignable to type '{ order: Order; }'.
Property 'key' does not exist on type '{ order: Order; }'.

payload.text:
**Pinpointing the Errors**

I'm currently focused on the `DeliveryView.tsx` component, specifically lines 228 and 248. The TypeScript compiler is flagging an issue: it seems I'm trying to assign a `key` property where it's not expected. The error message is clear – I need to ensure the data structure conforms to the `Order` type, without the extraneous `key`. I'll delve into the code to trace the source of this extra property and rectify it.


**Examining Component Interactions**

I'm now diving into the `OrderCard` component. The TypeScript compiler is complaining, and it's pointing at how `key` is being used within the rendering process of the `DeliveryView.tsx` component.  It seems I'm trying to assign a `key` prop, but TypeScript doesn't see it as part of the `OrderCard` component's props. I'll need to check the data flow from `DeliveryView` to `OrderCard` to understand where this is happening. The goal is a clean data flow where `OrderCard` only receives the data it needs via `order`.


**Clarifying Prop Handling**

I'm zeroing in on the `OrderCard` component again. I realized React handles the `key` prop differently for components declared inline. The error message made more sense now. I had the definition of `OrderCard` as `const OrderCard = ({ order }: { order: Order }) => (`. It seems the issue is that it's expecting `key` as a prop in the inline declaration. I've confirmed that the component does not need to explicitly handle the `key` prop. I can solve this by either defining `OrderCard` outside the main component or removing the explicit `key` prop altogether.


**Refining Component Keys**

I'm now revisiting the `<OrderCard>` component, specifically how I'm handling the `key` prop. I initially defined the component as an inline function, causing the compiler to flag an issue with the implicit `key`. I've realized React expects the `key` property on the rendered element. The component itself should not directly handle the key, but it needs to be provided when it's rendered, which I'm already doing. The key is now explicitly set within the component's enclosing `div` element. I'm confirming the `<OrderCard />` usage, ensuring keys are provided, as the original usage was correct.


**Fixing Component Definitions**

I'm now revisiting the `OrderCard` component one last time, focusing on its definition within `DeliveryView.tsx` (line 104). The core issue is that I've been defining `OrderCard` as a plain function. This causes the TypeScript compiler to complain about the missing `key` property. However, I've realized that by switching to `React.FC<{ order: Order }>`, I explicitly tell TypeScript that it's a React component, which includes handling of the `key` property internally. This solves the immediate error by making `key` an implicit part of the expected props. This resolves the issue and adheres to React best practices for component definition within functional components.


**Updating Component Definitions**

I've been wrestling with how I've defined the `OrderCard` component, and I've circled back to the React documentation to confirm the recommended approach for functional components in TypeScript. My prior approaches created issues with the `key` prop, due to how the `key` is handled implicitly by React. Now, I'm refactoring the definition by using `const OrderCard: React.FC<{ order: Order }> = ({ order }) => (`. This declaration explicitly states the component as a React Functional Component, leveraging its built-in handling of the `key` prop. This change should resolve the TypeScript compiler's complaint and ensure correct component rendering, aligning better with best practices.


**Defining as FC**

I've returned to the `OrderCard` component, and I've concluded that the cleanest solution, considering all the constraints, is to define it as a `React.FC<{ order: Order }>`. The issue boiled down to the explicit `key` React and JSX interplay in inline component definitions. Using `React.FC` properly addresses the compiler's complaint. This approach should satisfy React's requirement for a `key` while preventing the TypeScript error. This resolves the `key` issue without requiring externalizing the definition.


**Validating Key Placement**

I'm now back in the `DeliveryView.tsx` component, specifically on line 104 and how `<OrderCard>` is used, where I've removed the `key` from the `div` inside `OrderCard` and added it to `<OrderCard>` where it's called. My hypothesis is that the issue is in the type definition, not the usage of the key itself. So I'll double-check all component definitions and usages, making sure the `key` is present and correctly typed at the render site, which is the line where the component is rendered with the key attached.


**Verifying Component Keys**

I'm now revisiting the `<OrderCard>` component's key handling. I've been experimenting with moving the key, and I've circled back to checking the key prop within the `DeliveryView.tsx` component. The core problem appears to be a mismatch between the expected props for `OrderCard` and how the key is implicitly handled. I'll meticulously review the `DeliveryView`'s rendering of `<OrderCard>`, ensuring the key is correctly assigned. This should iron out all key-related issues.


**Optimizing Component Scope**

I'm now revisiting the `DeliveryView.tsx` file and considering moving the `OrderCard` definition outside to clarify its scope. However, this necessitates passing the `updateStatus` function as a prop, which I'm hesitant to do. The error seems to be due to defining the component inline; the simplest solution may be to define it as a standard function, which will implicitly resolve the key and type issue and make the code more readable.


**Simplifying Component Rendering**

I'm now revisiting the rendering of `OrderCard` within `DeliveryView.tsx`. Initially, I considered using `React.Fragment` to address the `key` prop issue, which did resolve the TypeScript error. Now, I'm verifying if that fix truly simplified the component's render, and it does! Removing the key from `div` fixes it too. With this approach, the code is both cleaner and type-safe. This streamlined approach seems to be the best solution.


**Finalizing Key Implementation**

I'm now revisiting the `<OrderCard>` component's key handling one last time. I've concluded the most effective and cleanest solution is using `React.Fragment` within the `DeliveryView.tsx` component. This ensures the `key` prop is correctly managed by React while maintaining clear code. The `OrderCard` component itself will receive only the props it needs, resolving the key-related TypeScript error definitively. This implementation also aligns with React's recommended practices for key management.


**Simplifying Key Management**

I'm now implementing the `renderOrderCard` approach I outlined. It seems cleaner. By renaming the component definition and usage to `renderOrderCard`, I'm hoping to sidestep the implicit `key` prop confusion. I've updated the component definition and its calls within `DeliveryView.tsx`. This change should eliminate the type error without adding unnecessary complexity, aligning better with React best practices for handling component rendering and keys when components are defined inline. I'm excited to see if this change yields the desired result of resolving the TypeScript error.


**Refactoring for Clarity**

I'm now revisiting the rendering of `OrderCard` with the specific aim of achieving more readable code. I've switched to a dedicated function `renderOrderCard` that handles the rendering and key assignment. With the key now handled within the `renderOrderCard` function, I'm confident that this approach minimizes potential React-related issues. The key is now explicitly set on the root element rendered by the `renderOrderCard` function. I'm hoping this will eliminate the underlying `key` type conflicts.


**Revising Key Placement**

I'm now back at the `<OrderCard>` component, and I've replaced it with a `renderOrderCard` function to fix the issue on line 228. This should simplify the `key` handling. I'm verifying the new setup to eliminate type errors.


**Simplifying Component Rendering**

I'm now revisiting the rendering of `OrderCard` within `DeliveryView.tsx`, confirming the `renderOrderCard` approach I outlined. By simplifying the render with this refactored function, the code is much cleaner, and no more TypeScript issues. The key is now handled internally. The streamlined approach is the best solution for clean and type-safe code.


**Reviewing Component Integration**

I'm now revisiting the rendering of `OrderCard` again, specifically the `key` prop within `DeliveryView.tsx`. I've confirmed that all instances use keys as required. I've re-examined the code, and everything appears to be in order. The type errors have been addressed, and the component integration is now sound. This check concludes the efforts on this component integration.


**Optimizing Component Scope**

I'm verifying that the inline function is now correct with the `renderOrderCard` change. With the function being so simple, the inline definition is ok now. I also reviewed the related code to see if there were any issues.


**Verifying State Management**

I'm now focusing on the `useEffect` hook within `DeliveryView.tsx`. Specifically, I'm checking the interaction between the `refresh` function, the `setInterval`, and `navigator.geolocation.clearWatch`. I'm verifying the logic to ensure that location updates are correctly stopped when the component unmounts and that the order refreshing is handled correctly, preventing memory leaks and maintaining up-to-date order data. I am also making sure that both `setActiveOrders` and `setCompletedOrders` are thread-safe and atomic.


**Examining Location Updates**

I'm now deep-diving into the `useEffect` hook in `DeliveryView.tsx`, focusing on the geolocation integration, particularly the interaction between `setInterval`, `navigator.geolocation.clearWatch`, and `watchIdRef.current`. I'm meticulously checking the logic to ensure that location tracking initiates only when there are active orders in transit, and that it's properly stopped when the component unmounts or no active orders are present. I'm also confirming that the `watchPosition` error handling is robust.


**Finalizing Location Integration**

I'm revisiting the `useEffect` hook in `DeliveryView.tsx` again, ensuring that all aspects of the geolocation integration are solid. I'm double-checking that the `watchPosition` error handling is complete, that the correct cleanup happens when the component unmounts, and the state updates and order refreshing are atomic. This ensures that no data inconsistencies can happen.


**Addressing Render Logic**

I'm now zeroing in on the `renderOrderCard` function. I'm verifying the structure and styling. I need to make sure the keys are being passed along to the function.


**Streamlining Order Display**

I'm now revising the `renderOrderCard` to enhance its presentation. I'm focusing on aligning the structure and styling of the order details. I'm double checking all the dynamic content is displaying correctly. I'm also reviewing the use of Tailwind classes, verifying that styles apply as intended, especially the dynamic styling based on order status and notes.


**Streamlining Order Buttons**

I'm now revising the buttons within `renderOrderCard`, targeting the `updateStatus` functionality and button styling. I've streamlined the logic using the ternary operator to render the appropriate button based on the order status. This should improve the readability and maintainability of the rendering logic. I'm verifying the styling and behavior of the buttons to ensure proper functionality and a consistent user experience.


**Polishing Button Styling**

I'm now refining the buttons in `renderOrderCard`, focusing on their visual appeal and alignment with the application's design principles. I'm verifying the application of Tailwind classes, specifically the hover states, to ensure smooth transitions and a consistent user experience. I'm also double-checking the visual hierarchy and spacing to improve overall readability.


**Improving the Display**

I'm now focusing on adding some CSS to the app. I'm going to start with a background color for the screen.


**Improving the Display**

Now, I'm integrating the CSS changes, especially the `bg-stone-50` background. I'm reviewing other Tailwind classes. I have the new background in `DeliveryView`.


**Analyzing Visual Refinements**

I'm now focusing on the visual display, specifically the styling and structure of the tab buttons in `DeliveryView.tsx`. I am aiming for enhanced consistency and visual cues with Tailwind classes. I am also planning on enhancing the structure and styling for clarity and user-friendliness in the UI.

**Validating Component Structure**

I'm now diving into the `DeliveryView` to clean up the code. I need to make sure the structure and styling of the component are correct and adhere to best practices for React components.


**Improving Empty States**

I'm now focusing on enhancing the empty state messaging within the application. I've added distinct messages and visual cues to signify no active or completed orders, making the interface more user-friendly. These changes ensure the empty state messages are consistent.


**Streamlining Order Display**

I'm finalizing the logic for order rendering, ensuring correct status indicators.  I've refined button styling and now will ensure display consistency across order types.

**Finalizing UI Polish**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the UI, paying close attention to visual consistency across all components.  The goal is a refined and professional look for the entire application, paying special focus to spacing and visual balance.


**Simplifying Code Readability**

I've streamlined the rendering of orders by using `map(renderOrderCard)` for in-transit and ready-to-pickup states. This makes the code more concise and easier to read, reducing unnecessary lines and visual clutter.


**Streamlining Order Rendering**

I'm now revising the rendering of orders in the `DeliveryView.tsx`. Initially I was planning to rename it, but now I'm making sure all order types use `map` with inline arrow functions. This ensures consistency and simplifies the code's visual structure.


**Finalizing UI Implementation**

Now I'm completely satisfied with the `DeliveryView` component. The `key` and rendering issues are resolved, the state management is robust, and the UI is refined and consistent. All aspects, from location tracking to order display, are now solid. The goal of a clean, functional, and user-friendly interface is fully achieved.

entries.description: Changed OrderCard from a component to a render function to fix TypeScript key prop error when used in a list.
